questions.scenario: A newly installed Jenkins controller has `Number of executors` set to 2 on the built-in node. The team also has three Linux agents connected. What is the safest production setting for the controller?

options.text: Leave the controller at 2 executors so small jobs can run there when agents are busy.
Set the controller's executor count to 0 so builds only run on agents.
Set every agent to 0 executors and run all jobs on the controller.
Keep one controller executor only for deploy jobs because they are important.

questions.explanation: The article's central rule is that the controller coordinates work but should not execute builds. Setting the built-in node to 0 executors keeps CPU, memory, workspace files, and build artifacts away from the controller process.

questions.scenario: A Jenkins build runs `npm ci && npm test`. Which part of the Jenkins architecture should execute those commands?

options.text: The controller JVM, because it owns the build queue.
An agent workspace, after the controller dispatches the job to an available executor.
`$JENKINS_HOME/jobs/`, because that is where job configuration lives.
The browser session of the user who clicked Build Now.

questions.explanation: Agents execute shell commands, Docker builds, and test suites in their workspaces. The controller decides which executor gets the job and streams logs, but the build steps belong on agents.

questions.scenario: An operator notices build artifacts collecting under `$JENKINS_HOME/workspace/` on the controller. Why is that a serious warning sign?

options.text: It means Jenkins has no agents configured and is storing normal controller metadata.
It suggests jobs are running on the built-in node, sharing disk with job configs, plugins, secrets, and build history.
It means every agent workspace is backed up automatically.
It proves the controller has too many labels.

questions.explanation: `$JENKINS_HOME` holds the controller's critical state. If build workspaces appear there, build output is competing with controller state for disk and increases the blast radius of a bad build script.

questions.scenario: Which responsibilities belong to the Jenkins controller rather than to an agent?

options.text: Manage the web UI and job queue.
Store credentials, plugin state, and job configuration.
Run `docker build` for application images.
Dispatch build instructions to available executors.

questions.explanation: The controller is the coordinator: UI, queue, credentials, plugins, job config, and dispatch. Heavy build commands such as `docker build` should run on agents.

questions.scenario: A controller crashes during the workday. Agents were running several builds, and `$JENKINS_HOME` is intact on disk. What should you expect?

options.text: All agents keep running builds independently and report results later.
Agents lose their coordinator, but job configs, build history, credentials, and node definitions can be recovered from `$JENKINS_HOME`.
The agents automatically become temporary controllers.
All Jenkins state is lost because agents own job configuration.

questions.explanation: The article stresses that `$JENKINS_HOME` is the controller's source of truth. Agents execute work, but the controller owns the persistent Jenkins state and coordination.
